en.extract:
The grey-headed canary-flycatcher (Culicicapa ceylonensis), sometimes known as the grey-headed flycatcher, is a species of small flycatcher-like bird found in tropical Asia. It has a square crest, a grey hood and yellow underparts. They are found mainly in forested habitats where they often join other birds in mixed-species foraging flocks. Pairs are often seen as they forage for insects by making flycatcher-like sallies and calling aloud. Several subspecies are recognized within their wide distribution range. In the past the genus Culicicapa was considered to be an Old World flycatcher but studies have found them to belong to a new family designated as the Stenostiridae or fairy flycatchers that include the African genera Stenostira and Elminia.


Description

The grey-headed canary-flycatcher is about 12–13 cm (4.7–5.1 in) long with a squarish grey head, a canary yellow belly and yellowish-green upperparts. They forage actively like flycatchers and perches in a very upright posture. The sexes are indistinguishable in plumage. They have a very flat bill (which gave it the earlier genus name of Platyrhynchus) which when seen from above look like an equilateral triangle and is fringed with long rictal bristles.
Across their range, populations differ in the shades of the colours and vary slightly in dimensions and several of these have been designated as subspecies. The nominate subspecies breeds in peninsular India in the hills of the Western Ghats, Nilgiris, central India and the Eastern Ghats (Lammasingi) and Sri Lanka. Subspecies calochrysea first described by Harry Oberholser in 1923 breeds along the Himalayas east to Myanmar and Thailand and winters across southern India. The nominate form is darker in shade. Subspecies antioxantha which was also described by Oberholser has a breeding range from southern Burma, Thailand through Malaysia to Java and Bali. The island population sejuncta described by Ernst Hartert in 1897 is found on Sumbawa, Flores and possibly on Lombok while connectens described by Bernhard Rensch in 1931 is restricted to the island of Sumba.
The grey-headed canary-flycatcher was earlier considered to be an Old World flycatcher in spite of its odd crest, colours and nature of vocalization. Molecular phylogeny studies however show that they are quite different and have been placed in the family Stenostiridae that is closely related to the tits and penduline-tits.


Habitat and distribution
This species breeds in upland to montane oak (Quercus) and other broadleaved forests and similar wooded areas in temperate to tropical southern Asia, from Pakistan, Central India, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka east to Indonesia and southern China. Many populations are resident, but some Himalayan birds are partial migrants that winter in peninsular India sometimes even occurring in arid habitats. Other populations may make altitudinal movements but in parts of the eastern Himalayas such as Bhutan, they have been found to occur all year round, and can be found up to and above 2,000 m ASL. During the non-breeding season, they are seen in the plains and lower elevations (September to March in India) when they prefer well wooded areas of relatively mature secondary forests, abandoned plantations and overgrown gardens, usually near water and streams including wooded gorges. They are very active and noisy throughout the day foraging at all levels of the forest.


Behaviour and ecology
The grey-headed canary-flycatcher is an insectivore and like flycatchers makes sallies for aerial insects from a low perch under the canopy of a tree. A pair may forage together and they will often join mixed-species foraging flocks. They breed in summer (April to June in India and possibly later in western China
). The nest, built by the female alone, is an unlined cup bound by cobwebs and often attached to and covered by moss. The nest is found placed against the mossy trunk of a tree or built on rocks or on a ledge in a mud bank. The typical clutch is three or four eggs. Hodgson's hawk-cuckoo has been observed parasitizing the brood of this species in northern Borneo.


References


External links

In Internet Bird Collection

The '''grey-headed canary-flycatcher''' ('''''Culicicapa ceylonensis'''''), sometimes known as the '''grey-headed flycatcher''', is a species of small [[Old World flycatcher|flycatcher]]-like [[bird]] found in tropical Asia. It has a square crest, a grey hood and yellow underparts. They are found mainly in forested habitats where they often join other birds in [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s. Pairs are often seen as they forage for insects by making flycatcher-like sallies and calling aloud. Several subspecies are recognized within their wide distribution range. In the past the genus ''[[Culicicapa]]'' was considered to be an [[Muscicapidae|Old World flycatcher]] but studies have found them to belong to a new family designated as the [[Stenostiridae]] or fairy flycatchers that include the African genera ''[[Stenostira]]'' and ''[[Elminia]]''.

==Description==
[[File:The Grey Headed Canary Flycatcher.jpg|thumb|upright|left|Nominate subspecies from India]]
The grey-headed canary-flycatcher is about {{cvt|12|-|13|cm|in}} long with a squarish grey head, a canary yellow belly and yellowish-green upperparts.<ref name="hbw.com">Clement, P. (2006). Grey-headed Canary-flycatcher (Culicicapa ceylonensis). In: del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A., Sargatal, J., Christie, D.A. & de Juana, E. (eds.) (2014). Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona. (retrieved from http://www.hbw.com/node/59108 on 8 March 2015).</ref> They forage actively like flycatchers and perches in a very upright posture. The sexes are indistinguishable in plumage.<ref name=hbk/> They have a very flat bill (which gave it the earlier genus name of ''Platyrhynchus'') which when seen from above look like an equilateral triangle and is fringed with long rictal bristles.<ref name=fbi2>{{cite book|author=Baker, EC Stuart|year=1924 |title=Fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 2|pages=254–255|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds2/bakerFBI2#page/n284/mode/1up|edition=2nd|publisher=Taylor and Francis|place=London}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia02oaterich#page/38/mode/2up|title=Fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 2|author=Oates, EW|pages=38–39|year=1890}}</ref>

Across their range, populations differ in the shades of the colours and vary slightly in dimensions and several of these have been designated as subspecies. The nominate subspecies breeds in peninsular India in the hills of the [[Western Ghats]], [[Nilgiri Hills|Nilgiris]], central India<ref>{{cite journal|author=Osmaston, BB |year=1922|title=Birds of Pachmarhi|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=28|issue=2|pages=453–459|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30113035#page/593/mode/1up}}</ref> and the [[Eastern Ghats]] (Lammasingi<ref>{{cite journal|last=Price|first=Trevor D |year=1979|title= The seasonality and occurrence of birds in the Eastern Ghats of Andhra Pradesh|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=76|issue=3|pages=379–422|url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48240774}}</ref>) and Sri Lanka. Subspecies ''calochrysea'' first described by [[Harry Church Oberholser|Harry Oberholser]] in 1923 breeds along the Himalayas east to Myanmar and Thailand and winters across southern India. The nominate form is darker in shade. Subspecies ''antioxantha'' which was also described by Oberholser has a breeding range from southern Burma, Thailand through Malaysia to Java and Bali. The island population ''sejuncta'' described by [[Ernst Hartert]] in 1897 is found on Sumbawa, Flores and possibly on Lombok while ''connectens'' described by [[Bernhard Rensch]] in 1931 is restricted to the island of Sumba.<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite book|pages=373–375|url=https://archive.org/stream/checklistofbirds111986pete#page/372/mode/2up/|title=Check-list of birds of the world. Volume XI|year=1986| last1=Mayr|first1=Ernst|last2=Traylor Jr.|first2=M.A.|last3=Watson|first3=GE|publisher=Museum of comparative Zoology|place= Cambridge, Massachusetts}}</ref><ref name=pcr/><ref>{{cite journal|journal=The Auk|volume =64|issue=4|year=1947|title= The Races of the Gray-Headed Flycatcher [Culicicapa Ceylonensis (Swainson)]|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v064n04/p0581-p0584.pdf|pages=581–584|doi=10.2307/4080717|last1=Deignan|first1=H. G.|jstor =4080717}}</ref>

The grey-headed canary-flycatcher was earlier considered to be an [[Old World flycatcher]] in spite of its odd crest, colours and nature of vocalization. [[Molecular phylogenetics|Molecular phylogeny]] studies however show that they are quite different and have been placed in the family [[Stenostiridae]] that is closely related to the tits and penduline-tits.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Phylogenetic relationships within Passerida (Aves: Passeriformes): A review and a new molecular phylogeny based on three nuclear intron markers|author1=Johansson, Ulf S|author2=Fjeldsa, J|author3=Bowie, RCK|year=2008|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=48|pages=858–876|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2008.05.029|url=http://www.nrm.se/download/18.7d9d550411abf68c801800015111/Johansson+et+al+Passerida+2008.pdf|issue=3|pmid=18619860|access-date=21 December 2012|archive-date=9 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210909190624/https://www.nrm.se/download/18.7d9d550411abf68c801800015111/Johansson+et+al+Passerida+2008.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Barker|first1=F. Keith|last2=Cibois|first2=Alice|last3=Schikler|first3=Peter A.|last4=Feinstein|first4=Julie |last5=Cracraft|first5=Joel |year=2004| title=Phylogeny and diversification of the largest avian radiation|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|volume=101|issue=30|pages=11040–11045|doi=10.1073/pnas.0401892101|pmid=15263073|pmc=503738|bibcode=2004PNAS..10111040B|doi-access=free}}</ref>
[[File:Grey-headed Canary Flycatcher.ogg|thumb|200px|[[Fraser's Hill]] ([[Malaysia]]), September 1997]]

==Habitat and distribution==
This species breeds in upland to [[Montane forest|montane]] [[oak]] (''Quercus'') and other [[broadleaved forest]]s and similar wooded areas in [[temperate]] to [[tropical]] southern [[Asia]], from [[Pakistan]], [[Central India]], [[Bangladesh]] and [[Sri Lanka]] east to [[Indonesia]] and southern [[China]]. Many populations are resident, but some [[Himalayas|Himalayan]] birds are [[bird migration|partial migrants]] that winter in peninsular India sometimes even occurring in arid habitats.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Joshua, J. Soni|author2=H. Joshi|author3=N. M. Joshi|author4=P. N. Deiva, O. |year=2005|title=Occurrence of Grey-headed Canary Flycatcher ''Culicicapa ceylonensis'' (Swainson) in Jamnagar district, Gujarat, India|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=102|issue=3|page=340| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48376005}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Whistler |first=Hugh|year=1914|title=The Grey-headed Flycatcher ''Culicicapa ceylonensis'', Swainson|journal= Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=22|issue=4|page=795|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30155836}}</ref> Other populations may make altitudinal movements but in parts of the eastern Himalayas such as [[Bhutan]], they have been found to occur all year round, and can be found up to and above 2,000 m [[Above mean sea level|ASL]].<ref name=pcr /> During the non-breeding season, they are seen in the plains and lower elevations (September to March in India) when they prefer well wooded areas of relatively mature secondary forests, abandoned plantations and overgrown gardens, usually near water and streams including wooded gorges. They are very active and noisy throughout the day foraging at all levels of the forest.<ref name="hbw.com"/>

==Behaviour and ecology==

The grey-headed canary-flycatcher is an [[insectivore]] and like flycatchers makes sallies for aerial insects from a low perch under the canopy of a tree. A pair may forage together and they will often join [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s.<ref>{{cite journal|title=The composition and spatial organisation of mixed-species flocks in a Sri Lankan rainforest|last1=Kotagama|first1=Sarath W.|last2=Goodale|first2=Eben|year=2004|journal=Forktail|volume=20|pages=63–70 |url=http://birdingasia.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/Kotagama-Flocks.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Partridge|first1=L.|last2=Ashcroft|first2=R. |year=1976|title=Mixed-species flocks of birds in hill forest in Ceylon.| journal=Condor|volume=78|issue=4|pages=449–453|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v078n04/p0449-p0453.pdf|doi=10.2307/1367093|jstor=1367093}}</ref> They breed in summer (April to June in India and possibly later in western China<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bangs|first=Outram|year=1932|title= Birds of western China obtained by the Kelley-Roosevelts expedition |journal=Field Mus. Nat. Hist. Zool. Ser.|volume=18|issue=11|pages=343–379|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsofwesternch1811bang#page/352/mode/1up}}</ref>
). The nest, built by the female alone, is an unlined cup bound by cobwebs and often attached to and covered by moss. The nest is found placed against the mossy trunk of a tree or built on rocks or on a ledge in a mud bank.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|last1=Rasmussen|first1=PC|last2=Anderton|first2=JC|year=2005|title=Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|page=387|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|place=Washington DC & Barcelona}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Inglis, Charles M |year=1949|title= Unrecorded nesting sites of the Greyheaded Flycatcher [Culicicapa ceylonensis ceylonensis (Swainson)]|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=48|issue=2|page=359|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48731847}}</ref> The typical [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]] is three or four eggs.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|pages=201–204|last1=Ali|first1=Salim|last2=Ripley|first2=S.D.|year=1996|edition=2nd|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 7. Laughing Thrushes to the Mangrove Whistler|publisher=Oxford University Press| place=New Delhi}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Wait, WE |year=1922|title=The passerine birds of Ceylon.|journal=Spolia Zeylanica |volume=12|pages= 22–194 [122]|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14917001#page/108/mode/1up}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/11260073#page/38/mode/1up|title=The nests and eggs of Indian Birds. Volume 2|author=Hume, AO|edition=2nd|editor=Oates, EW|publisher=R.H.Porter|place=London| year=1890|page=16}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Popular handbook of Indian birds|author=Whistler, Hugh|year=1949|edition=4th|publisher=Gurney and Jackson|place=London|url=https://archive.org/stream/popularhandbooko033226mbp#page/n165/mode/2up|pages=128–129}}</ref> [[Hodgson's hawk-cuckoo]] has been observed parasitizing the brood of this species in northern Borneo.<ref>{{cite journal|journal=Ibis|year=1890|title=On the Ornithology of Northern Borneo. Part V.|author=Sharpe, RB|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1890.tb06461.x|volume=32|pages=1–24|url=https://archive.org/stream/ibis621890brit#page/10/mode/2up/}}</ref>

zh.extract:
方尾鶲（学名：Culicicapa ceylonensis）为鶲科方尾鶲属的鸟类。该物种分布于亚洲南部各地，模式产地在斯里兰卡。


亚种
方尾鶲西南亚种（学名：Culicicapa ceylonensis calochrysea）。在中国大陆，分布于西藏、四川、甘肃、陕西、湖北、湖南、贵州、广西、云南、广东等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸。
方尾鶲指名亚种（学名：Culicicapa ceylonensis ceylonensis）
